title: Sensitive information and proxy features: privacy, fairness, data minimization
scope: Direct sensitive attributes; proxies (postcode, device type); embeddings that retain private information; removing vs. retaining protected attributes and the fairness effect; data minimization; 'improves accuracy' ≠ 'legitimate'.
key_concepts: Dropping sensitive attributes (race, gender) is not enough to ensure fairness because models can learn proxies.
A proxy feature is an outwardly neutral feature (like ZIP code) that heavily correlates with a sensitive attribute.
Learned embeddings can unintentionally encode and leak sensitive information present in the raw text or image.
Data minimization dictates that only strictly necessary features should be computed and retained.
definitions: Proxy feature: A feature that is not inherently sensitive but statistically correlates strongly with a protected attribute.
Data minimization: The principle of restricting the collection and use of data to only what is strictly necessary for a specific objective.
examples: Removing a 'gender' column but leaving 'shoe size' and 'height', which the model uses to reconstruct the missing gender variable and discriminate anyway.
relations_to_other_chapters: Re-emerges as a core design principle in Ch 17.
what_to_exclude: Complex mathematical definitions of fairness (equalized odds, demographic parity calculations).
